meta.title: Search box typing lag under product-card load
meta.summary: Typing feels sticky after the third character because urgent render work and search requests both fire on every keystroke.
signals: Large CPU cost in render and highlight logic
Every keystroke also triggers network work

context.symptom: Search input starts smooth, then each keystroke stalls for roughly 200ms once the result set grows.
context.userImpact: Users feel like the UI is dropping characters and abandon the search flow before refining their query.
context.environment: React 18 catalog page, 2,400 local items rendered as cards, server suggestions requested on every change.

evidence.title: RUM input delay
evidence.body: P95 input delay rises from 38ms to 212ms after the third character.

evidence.title: Profiler summary
evidence.body: Most time is inside filter + highlight logic before the browser paints.

evidence.title: Current handler

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: Which explanation best matches the evidence?
stages.helperText: Choose the failure mode that explains both the CPU spike and the janky typing.

options.label: Expensive filtering and highlighting run on the urgent input path, so every keystroke blocks paint.
options.feedback: Correct. The lag is dominated by synchronous work tied directly to the input event.

options.label: Card hover transitions are the main cause; JavaScript is mostly fine.
options.feedback: CSS can contribute, but the profiler points to JavaScript work before paint, not hover styling.

options.label: Global keyboard listeners are leaking and duplicating each keypress.
options.feedback: There is no signal of duplicated handlers. The evidence points to heavy work, not repeated events.

options.label: Network latency alone is causing the input to freeze.
options.feedback: Network can delay results, but it does not explain the CPU-heavy render path seen in the profiler.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Put the three highest-leverage debug steps first.
stages.helperText: The top three positions are scored. Move the best first steps to the top.

candidates.label: Profile a realistic typing session in React DevTools or Performance panel.
candidates.description: Confirm where urgent time is spent before changing architecture.

candidates.label: Mock network responses and isolate the client-side filter/highlight cost.
candidates.description: Separate CPU work from request latency.

candidates.label: Throttle CPU and network independently to see which pressure dominates.
candidates.description: Check whether the bottleneck is paint-blocking work or request latency.

candidates.label: Rewrite the suggestions endpoint before measuring the UI.
candidates.description: Large change with weak diagnostic value.

candidates.label: Add a debounce immediately and skip profiling.
candidates.description: Common fix, but weak as a first diagnostic step.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which fixes attack the real bottleneck without hiding the problem?
stages.helperText: Select all changes that belong in the first production-quality patch.

options.label: Debounce suggestion requests so network work is not fired on every single keystroke.
options.feedback: Correct. This cuts avoidable request churn and reduces work on the input path.

options.label: Move expensive derived filtering/highlighting off the urgent path with deferred rendering or equivalent scheduling.
options.feedback: Correct. The key is reducing blocking render work, not only shrinking request count.

options.label: Virtualize the result list so a large card grid does not rerender in full while typing.
options.feedback: Correct. Rendering fewer cards lowers total update cost once the query changes.

options.label: Increase the fetch timeout so requests stop failing under load.
options.feedback: This does not address input lag and can hide the real performance problem.

options.label: Disable StrictMode locally so typing feels smoother in development.
options.feedback: That can change dev noise, but it does not solve the production bottleneck.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best regression guard after the fix ships?
stages.helperText: Choose the signal that would catch this class of issue early next time.

options.label: Track search interaction latency and add a budget for typing responsiveness in the performance workflow.
options.feedback: Correct. This watches the actual user-facing failure mode instead of only backend timing.

options.label: Monitor only the search API p95 because slower requests caused the lag.
options.feedback: API timing matters, but it misses the blocking render work shown in the incident.

options.label: Have QA type quickly on release day and rely on that spot check.
options.feedback: Manual checks help, but they are too weak as the main guardrail.

options.label: Delete result highlighting permanently so the page can never regress again.
options.feedback: That throws away product behavior instead of building a durable performance guardrail.

scoreBands.label: Needs a tighter perf runbook
scoreBands.summary: You reached for fixes before isolating the urgent work on the typing path.

scoreBands.label: Good instincts, incomplete prioritization
scoreBands.summary: You found useful fixes, but your debug ordering or guardrail missed the main user-facing signal.

scoreBands.label: Production-ready diagnosis
scoreBands.summary: You isolated the blocking render path, chose layered fixes, and added a measurable regression guard.

idealRunbook: Profile the typing interaction before changing architecture.
Separate synchronous render cost from request latency.
Reduce urgent work first, then cut list rendering and request churn.
Track user-visible interaction latency after rollout.

teachingBlocks.title: What this incident tests
teachingBlocks.text: Frontend performance incidents are usually about choosing the right bottleneck first. Here, urgent render work mattered more than backend latency.

teachingBlocks.title: Strong answer signals
items: You distinguish CPU-bound lag from stale or slow network responses.
You pick a regression metric tied to the actual interaction.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: What would you measure first if this issue reappeared only on low-end mobile devices?
